items.title: Image classifier hidden layers (AlexNet-style CNN)
items.scenario: A convolutional network for ImageNet uses ReLU after every conv and fully connected hidden layer. After a conv block outputs feature maps, ReLU zeroes negative activations, keeping only positive edge and texture responses. Training converges in days instead of weeks compared to tanh hidden layers used in earlier networks.
items.explanation: ReLU's linear positive region preserves strong gradient flow for active neurons while producing sparse feature maps—standard practice in vision models since AlexNet (2012).

items.title: Neuron goes permanently silent mid-training
items.scenario: During MLP training on tabular data, one hidden unit's weights and bias shift so that z = Wx + b ≤ 0 for every training row. The unit outputs 0 always; backprop gives ∂L/∂w = 0 for that neuron. Validation accuracy plateaus while other units compensate. Switching to Leaky ReLU (α = 0.01) lets negative pre-activations still receive gradient and some units recover.
items.explanation: This is the dying ReLU problem in practice—zero gradient on the negative branch freezes weights until architecture or hyperparameters change.

items.title: PReLU in a segmentation backbone
items.scenario: A semantic segmentation model uses PReLU after each conv layer with one learnable α per output channel. Early channels learn α ≈ 0 (near-standard ReLU); channels detecting fine boundaries learn larger α, preserving weak negative responses that help edge localization.
items.explanation: PReLU lets each feature channel adapt its negative slope instead of forcing a single global α like Leaky ReLU.
